name: Software Pipelining

x.executionModel: Reorders a loop so that operations from several iterations overlap in flight, hiding instruction latency by starting later iterations before earlier ones finish.
x.dataOrg: Operates on the instruction schedule, not data layout; needs enough independent work and registers across iterations.
x.hwConstraints: Limited by register pressure, functional-unit throughput, and loop-carried dependencies.
x.compilerSupport: Performed by optimising compilers (modulo scheduling); hand-applied in tuned kernels / DSP.
x.portability: A compiler optimisation — portable in effect, though gains are microarchitecture-specific.
x.expectedBenefit: Higher instruction-level parallelism and functional-unit utilisation in tight loops.
divergenceRisks: Incorrect handling of the pipeline prologue/epilogue
x.workloadShapes: Long, dependency-light numeric / DSP loops with predictable trip counts.

def: A loop optimisation that overlaps instructions from multiple iterations so long-latency operations from one iteration are hidden behind independent work from others.
problem: Within a single loop iteration, instruction latencies stall the pipeline; there is not enough independent work to fill it.
mechanism: The scheduler (modulo scheduling) issues a steady-state kernel that contains operations belonging to several overlapping iterations, plus a prologue to fill and an epilogue to drain the pipeline — maximising functional-unit usage.
failures: Mishandled epilogue on non-multiple trip counts
myths: "Loop unrolling and software pipelining are the same" — unrolling replicates bodies; pipelining overlaps iterations
